description: Equation templates for predictive microbiology in dairy science: primary growth models, secondary (environmental) models, inactivation kinetics, and empirical relationships.

templates.equation_description: Logistic primary growth model for bacterial concentration over time, assuming no lag phase. The population grows from an initial level toward a maximum carrying capacity with growth rate governed by environmental conditions.

variables.name: time
variables.description: elapsed time since start of growth

variables.name: population at time t
variables.description: logarithmic concentration of the target organism at time t

assumptions: No lag phase; growth begins immediately at the given rate.
Logistic growth with a fixed maximum population density.
templates.difficulty_notes: Straightforward extraction when presented as a displayed equation with parameters defined nearby.

templates.equation_description: Full Baranyi primary growth model incorporating both a lag phase adjustment function and logistic saturation. The lag is modeled through an adjustment function A(t) that depends on the initial physiological state of the cells.
assumptions: Lag phase is modeled via an adjustment function A(t) = t + (1/mu) * ln(exp(-mu*t) + exp(-h0) - exp(-mu*t - h0)).
Parameter h0 represents the initial physiological state of the cells.
templates.difficulty_notes: Harder to extract because the adjustment function A(t) is typically defined separately, creating a multi-equation system.

templates.equation_description: Reparameterized Gompertz equation describing sigmoidal microbial growth as a function of time, with biologically meaningful parameters for maximum growth rate, lag time, and asymptotic population.

parameters.name: log increase from initial to maximum

assumptions: Sigmoidal growth curve; applicable when growth data show a clear lag, exponential, and stationary phase.
Reparameterized form from Zwietering et al. (1990) for biological interpretability.
templates.difficulty_notes: The nested exponentials make LaTeX extraction tricky; LLMs may flatten or rewrite the nesting.

templates.equation_description: Simple logistic growth equation for microbial populations, describing symmetric sigmoidal growth toward a carrying capacity.
assumptions: Symmetric growth curve around the inflection point.
No explicit lag phase.
templates.difficulty_notes: Simple and commonly encountered; straightforward to extract.

templates.equation_description: Dimensionless gamma factor representing the fractional effect of temperature on microbial growth rate, using the cardinal temperature model with minimum and optimal temperatures.
assumptions: Applicable for temperatures between T_min and T_opt.
Below T_min, gamma(T) = 0 (no growth).
Squared cardinal model following Ratkowsky or Zwietering convention.
templates.difficulty_notes: Often appears as part of a set of gamma factors; extraction must identify it as one component of a multi-factor model.

templates.equation_description: Dimensionless gamma factor representing the effect of pH on microbial growth rate, using minimum, optimum, and maximum pH values in a cardinal parameter model.
assumptions: Growth rate is zero at pH_min and pH_max.
Growth rate is maximal at pH_opt.
Assumes independent, multiplicative effect of pH on growth rate.
templates.difficulty_notes: The nested parentheses in numerator and denominator can cause extraction errors if not carefully parsed.

templates.equation_description: Linear cardinal model describing the effect of water activity on microbial growth rate, scaling from zero at the minimum water activity to one at pure water.
assumptions: Linear relationship between a_w and growth rate fraction.
a_w = 1 corresponds to full growth potential from this factor.
templates.difficulty_notes: Simple formula but the subscript notation (a_{w,min}) is a common extraction challenge.

templates.equation_description: The gamma concept expresses the effective growth rate as the product of the maximum growth rate and individual dimensionless gamma factors for each environmental variable, assuming multiplicative and independent effects.

parameters.name: maximum specific growth rate under optimal conditions

assumptions: Environmental factors have independent, multiplicative effects on growth rate.
Each gamma factor ranges from 0 (complete inhibition) to 1 (optimal conditions).
typical_organisms: any organism modeled with cardinal parameter approach
templates.difficulty_notes: This equation ties together multiple gamma factors; extraction must identify it as a composite equation and link to the individual gamma definitions.

templates.equation_description: Empirical square-root model relating the square root of growth rate to temperature, widely used in predictive microbiology as a simple secondary model.
assumptions: Linear relationship between sqrt(mu) and temperature in the suboptimal range.
T_min is a conceptual (extrapolated) value, not necessarily the observed minimum.
templates.difficulty_notes: Simple formula but the square root on the left-hand side is unusual and may confuse extractors.

templates.equation_description: Simple exponential growth equation for the log phase of microbial growth, before the population approaches carrying capacity.
assumptions: Unlimited resources; no carrying capacity constraint.
Applicable only during the exponential (log) phase.
templates.difficulty_notes: Very simple; often appears as a component of a larger model rather than a standalone equation.

templates.equation_description: Empirical linear relationship relating water activity of a dairy product to its water-phase salt concentration.
assumptions: Linear approximation valid for typical cheese salt concentrations.
Derived from empirical measurements on dairy products.
templates.difficulty_notes: Parameters are numeric constants embedded in the equation rather than named symbols; extraction must identify them.

templates.equation_description: First-order thermal inactivation kinetics for bacterial spores or vegetative cells during heat treatment, describing log-linear decline in viable count.
assumptions: First-order inactivation kinetics.
D-value is constant at a given temperature.
templates.difficulty_notes: Simple but may appear with temperature-dependent D values (z-value model) as a companion equation.

templates.equation_description: Describes how the decimal reduction time (D-value) changes with temperature, where z is the temperature increase required to reduce D by one log cycle.
assumptions: Log-linear relationship between D and temperature.
Applicable within the relevant temperature range for the organism.
templates.difficulty_notes: Often paired with the log-linear inactivation model; extraction should link the two.

templates.equation_description: Non-linear inactivation model using the Weibull distribution to describe microbial death curves that exhibit tailing or shoulder effects.

parameters.name: scale parameter (time for first log reduction)

assumptions: p < 1 indicates concave-downward (tailing) survival curves.
p > 1 indicates concave-upward (shoulder) survival curves.
p = 1 reduces to the log-linear model.
templates.difficulty_notes: The fraction N(t)/N_0 on the left-hand side and the exponent p on the right make this equation structurally distinct.

templates.equation_description: Relates microbial lag phase duration to storage temperature using an inverse power-law or exponential relationship, commonly used in shelf-life prediction models.
assumptions: Lag duration is inversely proportional to growth rate.
h0 is a strain-specific parameter representing the work needed to adapt to new conditions.
templates.difficulty_notes: The parameter h0 is often described in text rather than displayed in a parameter table.

templates.equation_description: Defines product shelf life as the time for the microbial population to reach a spoilage threshold concentration from a given initial contamination level.
assumptions: Exponential growth assumed throughout shelf life.
Spoilage occurs when a threshold concentration is reached.
No lag phase considered.
templates.difficulty_notes: Simple formula but the dual role of N_spoil (as both a variable and a parameter) can confuse extraction.

templates.equation_name: defect probability from Monte Carlo simulation
templates.equation_description: Estimates the proportion of production units exhibiting a defect by comparing simulated microbial concentrations against a threshold, used in risk assessment models.
assumptions: Each iteration represents an independent production unit with stochastically sampled parameters.
Indicator function returns 1 if the concentration exceeds the threshold.
templates.difficulty_notes: The indicator function notation and summation make this a harder extraction target.

templates.equation_description: Extended cardinal temperature model with minimum, optimum, and maximum temperatures, applicable across the full growth range including super-optimal temperatures.
templates.equation_latex: \gamma(T) = \frac{(T - T_{\max})(T - T_{\min})^2}{(T_{\mathrm{opt}} - T_{\min})\left((T_{\mathrm{opt}} - T_{\min})(T - T_{\mathrm{opt}}) - (T_{\mathrm{opt}} - T_{\max})(T_{\mathrm{opt}} + T_{\min} - 2T)\right)}
assumptions: Growth rate is zero at T_min and T_max, maximal at T_opt.
Asymmetric response around the optimum.
Rosso et al. (1993) cardinal model.
templates.difficulty_notes: Complex nested parentheses in the denominator; high difficulty for LaTeX extraction.
